text: whether schools met our minimum standards for Montessori inclusion. Two criteria were initially more strict (i.e., 75% or more teachers trained; at least 20 children per classroom) but following interviews were relaxed to include more schools in the study. The final implementation criteria for school inclusion were thus: o At least 66% of the lead Primary classroom teachers are trained by one of the two most prominent Montessori teacher training organizations, the Association Montessori Internationale (AMI) or the American Montessori Society (AMS). One school was excluded on this basis. e No more than two adults, the trained teacher and a non-teaching assistant, in the classroom on a regular basis. No school was excluded on this basis. e Classrooms are mixed-age, with at least 18 children ranging from 3 to 6 years old. Five schools did not mix ages so were excluded. e At least a 2-hour uninterrupted free choice period every day. Five schools were excluded on this basis. ¢ Each classroom has at least 80% of the complete set of roughly 150 Montessori Primary materials, and fewer than 5% of the materials available to children in the classroom are not Montessori materials. No school was excluded for failing to meet this criterion. The research team determined that 47 public Montessori schools were eligible for the study under the relaxed criteria. However, 22 of them did not have a waitlist of at least five students in the 2021-22 school year; for most this was unusual and likely due to COVID-19 which reduced public school enrollment nationwide. Of the remaining 25 eligible schools, one later withdrew when no enrolled students consented, leaving 24 schools. (Because they were contacted, the 12 children who applied to the withdrawn school are included in the total N in the Analytic Sample CONSORT chart, Figure S2).
